project.name: AISim Hydroponics Initiative — Community Growing Guide
project.owner: Simeon Reid Jr. — Recipe Labs / Eleven Views / AISim

deliverables.description: Step-by-step interactive growing guide with crop planner, cost calculator, pH tracker, and maintenance reminders. PWA-enabled for offline use.
Large-text, high-contrast printed guide for elderly family and community members. Simple language, numbered steps, big diagrams.
Bold visual cards with one concept per slide. For RichOff AI content brand.

audience.primary: Family first — elderly, people with disabilities, kids
audience.tertiary: Social media audience via RichOff AI

print_override.note: PDF uses LIGHT theme for print readability

carousel_override.note: Social slides use HIGH CONTRAST dark theme

crop_data.name: Lettuce
crop_data.notes: Best first crop — most forgiving. Harvest outer leaves for continuous production.

visual_stages.description: Tiny dark seeds placed in rockwool cube or net pot
First two small round leaves (cotyledons) emerge
True leaves develop, 2-3 inches tall
Full rosette forming, leaves spreading out
Full head, outer leaves ready to pick

crop_data.name: Basil
crop_data.notes: Pinch above leaf nodes for bushy growth. Enormous cost savings vs grocery.

visual_stages.description: Small black seeds, may form gel coating when wet
Cotyledons emerge, stem visible
First true leaves — classic basil shape
Multiple branches after first pinch
Regular pinching produces continuous harvest

crop_data.name: Callaloo
crop_data.notes: Caribbean staple. 30-50% faster than soil. 61% higher antioxidants hydroponically. Cut-and-come-again harvesting. Nearly unavailable fresh in Western MA.
crop_data.cultural_note: Essential ingredient in Trinidadian callaloo, Jamaican callaloo soup, and many Caribbean green dishes. Growing this fresh is a major advantage for Pan Road Catering.

visual_stages.description: Tiny round seeds scattered on growing medium
Red-green stems with small leaves
Upright growth, leaves broadening
Cut stems 6-8 inches, leaves are tender
New shoots from cut stems — harvest again

crop_data.name: Culantro
crop_data.notes: PREFERS SHADE — lower light needs = lower electricity cost. Nearly impossible to source fresh in Western MA. Biggest competitive advantage crop for Pan Road Catering. Slow germination needs patience — soak seeds 24hr before planting, keep 80-85°F. Once established, produces continuously for months.
crop_data.cultural_note: Essential for green seasoning, sofrito, and chutney across Trinidad, Guyana, Jamaica, and Puerto Rican cooking. 10x stronger flavor than cilantro.

visual_stages.description: Soak tiny seeds in warm water 24 hours
Press seeds into moist rockwool or surface of medium
Patience — nothing visible yet, keep moist and warm
Tiny green shoots finally emerge
Serrated (sawtooth) leaves forming rosette
Outer leaves 6-8 inches, harvest from outside in

crop_data.name: Scotch Bonnet Pepper
crop_data.notes: START SEEDS EARLY — 3-4 month lead time. Hand-pollinate indoors with small brush or shake plants. Plants become MORE productive as perennials in year 2+. Needs dedicated 5-gallon bucket for root space. Needs strongest light in the system.
crop_data.cultural_note: Backbone of Caribbean hot sauce, jerk seasoning, pepper sauce. Growing your own means unlimited supply for Pan Road Catering.

visual_stages.description: Small flat seeds on heat mat at 80-85°F
Slow — thin green stem emerges
First true leaves, 3-4 inches tall
Bushy plant 12-18 inches, branching
Small white flowers — hand pollinate these!
Green peppers forming
Peppers turn yellow/orange/red — ready to pick

crop_data.name: Thyme
varieties: Big leaf thyme / Cuban oregano (Plectranthus amboinicus)
crop_data.notes: 386% more fresh weight vs outdoor growing. Two important varieties for Caribbean cooking: common thyme (Thymus vulgaris) AND 'big leaf thyme' which is actually Cuban oregano (Plectranthus amboinicus) — a completely different plant. Cuban oregano roots easily from cuttings in water.

visual_stages.description: Tiny seeds OR stem cutting in water
Roots developing (cuttings root faster)
Bushy growth, aromatic leaves
Snip stems — plant continues growing

crop_data.name: Green Onions
crop_data.notes: Regrows from base after cutting. Can also start from grocery store scallion roots — just place in water. Fastest path to harvest for new growers.

visual_stages.description: Seed OR grocery store root end in net pot
Green shoots growing upward rapidly
6-8 inch greens ready — cut above white base
New growth from same base — harvest again

crop_data.name: Lemongrass
crop_data.start_method: Grocery store stalk rooted in water
crop_data.notes: 92% more production than soil-grown. Start from grocery store stalks — look for ones with intact base. Root in glass of water for 2-3 weeks before transplanting to DWC. Needs larger container.

visual_stages.description: Fresh grocery stalk trimmed, placed in water glass
White roots emerging from base in water
Move to DWC bucket with LECA
New green blades growing from center
Outer stalks pulled — inner ones keep growing

crop_data.name: Mint
crop_data.notes: Extremely vigorous grower — keep in its own container or it takes over. Great for drinks and Caribbean desserts.

system_comparison.name: Deep Water Culture (DWC)
system_comparison.how_it_works: Plant roots sit in oxygenated nutrient solution inside buckets. An air pump provides constant oxygen through air stones — like a fish tank for plants.
system_comparison.recommended_for: BEST ALL-AROUND STARTER. Recommended for this project.

system_comparison.name: Kratky Method (Passive)
system_comparison.how_it_works: Plants sit in net pots on sealed containers of nutrient solution. No pump, no electricity, no moving parts. As roots drink, an air gap forms naturally.
crop_compatibility: Herbs, leafy greens, green onions — NOT fruiting plants
system_comparison.recommended_for: BEST FOR ELDERLY/DISABILITY — simplest possible system. Set and forget.

system_comparison.name: NFT (Nutrient Film Technique)
system_comparison.how_it_works: A thin stream of nutrients flows through tilted channels. Roots sit in the stream and absorb nutrients as they pass by.
crop_compatibility: Leafy greens, herbs — NOT large fruiting plants
system_comparison.recommended_for: Best plant density per dollar. Good for scaling to catering supply.

system_comparison.name: Ebb-and-Flow (Flood and Drain)
system_comparison.how_it_works: A timer periodically floods a grow tray with nutrient solution, then drains it back to a reservoir. Roots get both nutrients and oxygen in cycles.
system_comparison.lava_rock_note: BEST MATCH for lava rocks — flooding/draining complements their drainage properties

system_comparison.name: Vertical Tower
system_comparison.how_it_works: Plants sit in stacked rings or pockets on a central column. Nutrient solution drips from the top and cascades down through all plant sites.
crop_compatibility: Greens, herbs, strawberries — limited for large plants
system_comparison.recommended_for: Best space efficiency. What you saw in the photo.

growing_media_comparison.name: LECA / Expanded Clay Pebbles
cons: Can float in deep water initially

growing_media_comparison.name: Lava Rock
growing_media_comparison.recommendation: Good budget option for ebb-and-flow. Use smaller, tumbled pieces. ALWAYS rinse until water runs clear.
tips: Select smaller pieces to reduce root damage
Rinse 3-5 times until water runs clear
Test pH before adding to system
Best for ebb-and-flow where flooding/draining cycle complements drainage

growing_media_comparison.name: Rockwool
growing_media_comparison.recommendation: Industry standard for seed starting. Not for ongoing growing medium.

growing_media_comparison.name: Coco Coir
growing_media_comparison.recommendation: Best water retention. Needs calcium-magnesium supplement. Degrades over time.

growing_media_comparison.name: Perlite
growing_media_comparison.recommendation: Best in blends. Floats in flood systems. Creates harmful dust — wear mask.

items.name: 4-bucket DWC system
items.product: VIVOSUN or DIY 5-gal buckets + net pot lids

items.name: Seeds
items.product: Lettuce, basil, callaloo, culantro, scotch bonnet, thyme

items.name: Miscellaneous
items.product: Measuring cups, syringes, spray bottle, labels

monthly_operating.electricity_note: Eversource all-in residential rate, West Springfield MA

phase4.label: Phase 4 — Community Expansion
phase4.trigger: Consistently producing 15+ lbs/week AND demand exceeds capacity

tasks: Check water level — top off if low
Quick visual check for wilting or pests
Complete nutrient solution change (drain, clean, refill)
Check for root tangles, trim if needed
Adjust light height for plant growth

troubleshooting.fix: Check roots, lower water temp below 75°F
Cover all light leaks, use opaque containers
Keep top of medium dry, use yellow sticky traps

build_stages_dwc.title: Setting Up Your DWC System — 10 Steps
build_stages_dwc.description: Visual build guide for a 4-bucket Deep Water Culture system

steps.title: Gather your supplies
steps.description: Lay out all equipment on a clean surface. You need: 4 buckets with lids, air pump, 4 air stones, tubing, LECA, net pots, nutrients, pH kit, timer.
steps.illustration_notes: Birds-eye flat lay of all components labeled. Clean, organized.

steps.title: Prepare the buckets
steps.description: If using DIY buckets, cut holes in lids to fit your net pots. Hole should be snug — net pot rim sits on lid surface.
steps.illustration_notes: Cross-section showing bucket, lid with hole, net pot fitting into hole.

steps.title: Rinse your LECA
steps.description: Put clay pebbles in a colander or mesh bag. Rinse under running water until water runs clear. This removes dust that can clog your system.
steps.illustration_notes: Hands holding colander under faucet, water running through clay pebbles.

steps.title: Set up the air system
steps.description: Connect air stones to tubing. Connect tubing to air pump (one tube per stone, one stone per bucket). Place air stones in bottom of each bucket.
steps.illustration_notes: Exploded diagram: air pump → tubing (with arrows showing flow) → air stones in bucket bottom.

steps.title: Fill buckets with water
steps.description: Fill each bucket with water to about 1 inch below the net pot. Use water that has sat out 24-48 hours to remove chlorine, or use a carbon filter.
steps.illustration_notes: Side-view cross-section showing water level relative to net pot position. Arrow marking '1 inch gap'.

steps.title: Mix nutrients
steps.description: Add FloraMicro FIRST (2.5mL per gallon), stir. Then add FloraGro (2.5mL), stir. Then FloraBloom (2.5mL), stir. Always add Micro first.
steps.illustration_notes: 3 bottles labeled clearly, pouring into bucket in numbered order. Measuring syringe shown.

steps.title: Test and adjust pH
steps.description: Dip pH meter in solution. Target: 5.8-6.2. If too high, add pH Down drops. If too low, add pH Up. Retest after each adjustment.
steps.illustration_notes: pH meter in bucket showing digital readout. pH Down bottle with dropper. Scale showing 5.8-6.2 'sweet spot' highlighted green.

steps.title: Plant your seeds or seedlings
steps.description: Place a rockwool cube with seed into the net pot, or nestle a seedling's roots through the net pot. Fill around with LECA pebbles to support the plant.
steps.illustration_notes: Cross-section of net pot: LECA pebbles around a small seedling, roots dangling below into water.

steps.title: Set up your light
steps.description: Hang or mount the LED panel 18-24 inches above the tops of the net pots. Plug into timer. Set timer: 14 hours ON, 10 hours OFF.
steps.illustration_notes: Side view: LED panel hanging from adjustable cables above 4 buckets. Timer plugged into wall showing on/off cycle.

steps.title: Turn on and monitor
steps.description: Plug in air pump (runs 24/7 — no timer needed). Turn on light. Check for bubbles in each bucket. Your system is now running! Check pH daily.
steps.illustration_notes: Happy completed system: 4 buckets under light, bubbles visible in water, small plants in net pots. Checkmark overlay.

build_stages_kratky.title: Setting Up a Kratky Mason Jar — 6 Steps
build_stages_kratky.description: The simplest possible hydroponic system — ideal for elderly and disability access

steps.title: Get a wide-mouth mason jar and net pot lid
steps.description: Any quart or half-gallon wide-mouth jar works. Net pot lids fit standard mason jar openings — buy online for about $2-3 each.
steps.illustration_notes: Mason jar with net pot lid sitting next to it. Simple, clean.

steps.title: Fill jar with nutrient water
steps.description: Mix nutrients in water (same Flora Trio recipe). Adjust pH to 5.8-6.2. Fill jar to the top.
steps.illustration_notes: Side view of jar filled with slightly tinted water. pH meter testing.

steps.title: Add LECA and plant to net pot
steps.description: Put a few LECA pebbles in the net pot. Nestle your seedling in. Fill around with more LECA. Roots should dangle below the pot.
steps.illustration_notes: Cross-section: net pot with LECA and small plant, roots hanging down.

steps.title: Place net pot on jar
steps.description: Set the net pot lid on the jar. The bottom of the net pot and roots should touch the water. That's it — no pump needed.
steps.illustration_notes: Assembled jar with plant. Arrow showing roots touching water level.

steps.title: Place under light and wait
steps.description: Put the jar under your LED light or on a bright windowsill. The plant drinks the water over time, and an air gap naturally forms — this gives roots oxygen.
steps.illustration_notes: Jar under LED strip light on a shelf. Side view showing air gap forming between water and net pot over time.

steps.title: Check once a week
steps.description: Top off with pH-adjusted nutrient water when level gets low. That's the entire maintenance routine. Harvest when ready!
steps.illustration_notes: Person pouring water from a small jug into the jar. Simple, relaxed.

web_app.keyboard: full tab navigation, enter/space to activate, escape to close
web_app.screen_reader: semantic HTML5, ARIA landmarks, alt text on all visuals, live regions for dynamic content
web_app.offline: PWA with service worker for offline access

font_recommendations.body: Inter — high x-height, open counters, good for elderly readers
font_recommendations.heading: Oswald — high legibility at large sizes
font_recommendations.data: JetBrains Mono or Tabular Lining figures in Inter

elderly_specific: No hover-only interactions — everything accessible via click/tap
Persistent navigation — no hamburger menus on large screens
Large, clearly labeled buttons with icon + text (never icon-only)
Avoid blue-yellow color combinations (hardest to distinguish with aging eyes)
High contrast mode toggle in settings
Text zoom support up to 200% without layout breakage
Simple language — one idea per sentence, active voice

recommended_components.library: Radix UI primitives + custom styling (best accessibility out of the box)
recommended_components.avoid: Material UI (overly complex for this use case)

pdf.contrast: Black text on white background, gold accents for headings
pdf.images: All diagrams have text descriptions below them
pdf.binding: Left margin extra 0.25 inch for staple binding

carousel.contrast: White or gold text on dark background
carousel.safe_zone: 100px from all edges for platform UI elements

cultural_notes.spanish: Many Caribbean Spanish speakers — use Caribbean Spanish, not Castilian
cultural_notes.haitian_creole: Significant Haitian community in Springfield MA
cultural_notes.caribbean_english: Use familiar Caribbean terminology (shadow beni, not culantro; callaloo, not amaranth)

web_inspiration.name: Epic Gardening
web_inspiration.what_to_learn: Progressive disclosure — simple overview first, expand for details. Clean card-based layout. Strong CTAs.

web_inspiration.name: Lettuce Grow
web_inspiration.what_to_learn: Beautiful product photography integration. Growing guides with timeline visualizations. Mobile-first responsive.

web_inspiration.name: Rise Gardens
web_inspiration.what_to_learn: Seed-to-harvest tracking interface. Clean data visualization for growing conditions.

web_inspiration.name: Gardyn
web_inspiration.what_to_learn: AI-powered growing recommendations. App-like web experience. Camera monitoring integration.

web_inspiration.name: UMN Extension Hydroponics
web_inspiration.what_to_learn: Academic credibility with accessible writing. Data tables with proper citations. Trusted by institutions.

web_inspiration.name: NoSoilSolutions
web_inspiration.what_to_learn: Real beginner perspective. Honest product reviews. Step-by-step tutorials with photos.

social_media_inspiration.what_works: Quick tips in 15-30 seconds. Before/after transformations. Bold text overlays on video.
Timelapse of growth. Clean aesthetic. Product integration.
Educational carousels. Step-by-step photo guides. Data-rich infographics.

illustration_style.reference: IKEA assembly manual style — minimal text, clear numbered visuals, universal understanding
diagram_types_needed: System cross-section (bucket cutaway showing water, roots, net pot, air stone, LECA)
Component exploded view (all parts labeled and numbered)
Water cycle flow diagram (reservoir → pump → plant → drain → reservoir)
Growth timeline horizontal bar chart (seed → sprout → veg → harvest for each crop)
pH scale visual (color-coded 4.0 to 8.0 with sweet spot highlighted)
Nutrient mixing sequence (3 bottles in order with measurements)
Space layout diagram (2x4 ft setup from above)
Plant spacing guide (top-down view of bucket arrangement)

web_app.styling: Tailwind CSS with custom design tokens matching brand
web_app.state: React hooks (useState, useReducer) — no external state management needed
web_app.data: Static JSON (no database needed for v1)
key_pages: /  — Landing with hero, quick start, crop highlights
/crops  — Crop explorer with filtering and detail cards
/care  — Maintenance schedule with checklist (saveable to localStorage alternative)
interactive_features: Crop planner: select crops → see space, cost, timeline requirements
Cost calculator: input budget → see recommended system + crop selection
pH guide: interactive pH scale with tap-to-learn zones
Harvest tracker: check off harvests, see running yield totals
Maintenance reminders: opt-in browser notifications for daily/weekly tasks

pdf.tool: ReportLab (Python) or Puppeteer screenshot of /print page
sections: Cover page with project name and community branding
What is hydroponics? (1 page, simple explanation with diagram)
What you need — shopping list with pictures (2 pages)
Step-by-step setup guide (3-4 pages with large diagrams)
What to grow — crop cards (1-2 pages)
Weekly care checklist (1 page — designed to photocopy and hang on wall)
Back cover with QR code to web app
